According to the *Law of Conservation of Energy*, energy cannot be created or destroyed but it can be converted from one form to another. For instance, in the previous example, energy stored in the food we eat was converted to kinetic (mechanical) energy to provide us with the energy to exercise. Conversion of energy from one form to another also releases *heat* energy, which is why our bodies can get very hot during exercise. Both muscle and body (core) temperature can rise by 5 and 3°C, respectively, during intense exercise (Drust *et al*., 2005).

The chemical reactions which take place in our bodies are of two main types:

## Definition

The Law of Conservation of Energy states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, but it can be converted from one form to another.

## Core Principles

- Energy is neither created nor destroyed; it only changes form.
- Energy conversions in biological systems transform chemical energy (e.g., stored in food) into kinetic (mechanical) energy used for movement and exercise.
- Every conversion of energy from one form to another also releases *heat* energy as a by-product.

## Sub-concepts

- **Chemical-to-mechanical energy conversion**: Energy stored in food is converted into kinetic (mechanical) energy that powers muscle contraction during exercise.
- **Heat release during energy conversion**: A portion of energy converted between forms is dissipated as heat.

_(尚無內容)_

## Practical Applications

- During intense exercise, the heat released from energy conversion raises muscle temperature by up to 5°C and body (core) temperature by up to 3°C (Drust *et al*., 2005).
- Understanding energy conservation explains why thermoregulation (e.g., sweating, increased blood flow to skin) is essential during prolonged or intense physical activity.

## Related Concepts

- Kinetic (mechanical) energy
- Chemical energy
- Heat / thermal energy
- Thermoregulation during exercise
- Metabolic chemical reactions (anabolic and catabolic)
